title: Cat Over-Grooming and Bald Spots: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat licking until they have bald spots? Learn about psychogenic alopecia, urgency levels, and how to help your feline friend today.
content_html:
What is Obsessive Grooming?

Obsessive grooming, medically referred to as psychogenic alopecia or over-grooming, occurs when a cat licks, bites, or chews their fur to the point of causing hair loss. This behavior is often a response to either physical discomfort—such as skin allergies, parasites, or pain—or emotional distress like anxiety and stress. You may notice thinning hair or completely bald patches, often on the belly, inner thighs, or forelegs.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for obsessive grooming is typically Low. While it is concerning to see your cat lose fur, it is rarely a life-threatening condition. You should schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian to investigate the cause. However, if you notice the skin is bleeding, oozing, or smells foul, this may indicate a secondary infection that needs more prompt medical attention.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, high-quality photograph of the bald spots and the surrounding skin is an essential step in the triage process. A photo allows a veterinary professional to assess the pattern of hair loss and the condition of the skin (checking for redness or lesions) without the cat needing to be present. It also helps track the progression of the area over time, making it easier to determine if the treatment plan is working.

faqs.question: Can fleas cause obsessive grooming?
faqs.answer: Yes, flea allergy dermatitis is one of the most common reasons cats over-groom. Even a single flea bite can cause an intense allergic reaction leading to excessive licking.

faqs.question: Is my cat doing this because they are stressed?
faqs.answer: It is possible. Cats are sensitive to environmental changes, such as moving house or a new pet. They may use grooming as a self-soothing mechanism to cope with anxiety.

faqs.question: Will the hair grow back?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes. Once the underlying cause—whether it is medical or behavioral—is identified and managed, the hair follicles usually recover and the fur regrows.
